I was skeptical when i saw the box. "We don't need to turn the wii-motes into a gun. How good can the pack-in be?" But then I had second thoughts and bought it. We've been playing it all night. I was a Duck Hunt fan and the rail shooting levels are fantastic - like the first time you played Mrs. PacMan after being a PacMan fan but so much better. There's a mix of scrolling, rail and 1st person shooting levels set in the Twilight Princess world that can be played in order or on their own. If you get a Wii for the holidays, pick this up as well. I can tell there's no point in even taking the wii-mote out of the zapper. The family will be playing this just about everyday, despite our having about a dozen other Wii titles. My 3 year old was delighted when she beat my high score. In my defense it would have been higher but I hit a live chicken. My 8 year old informed me "Mom! You can't shoot LIVE things!!!" Who knew? It was so much fun blasting the fruit balloons.... and the skeletons.... I'd defend my record, but my husband and brother have been playing for the last four hours and I can't get near it. Well worth the 20 - if they'd sold the game alone I would have paid more.

Grand Theft Auto 3 GTA3 is being released for PC after much speculation that it might not. I am in line for my copy. As it is not on PC yet, I played the Playstation 2 PS2 version. Not knowing anything about this game, I picked up the control expecting to play some kind of racing game. And then... I am standing on a bridge in a prison jump suit. My buddy, who is shot, is climbing into a car before me. He tells me to drive, he "Knows a guy" who can help us. Without pause, I climb into the car and I am driving. I can go anywhere in this city! I can stop ANYWHERE in this city and get out of my car. I can car-jack anyone's car I want. Some are junk, some are hotrods and some are fire-trucks! I could choose to work my way up the ranks of the mob. Or, I could just choose to go around town and cause trouble! It was like playing a video game for the first time. All I wanted to do was explore this rich world and story-line. And that is exactly what I plan to do when I get my PC Version. I highly recomend this GTA3 for adult gamers. If you are a parent, be aware, this game contains very mature subject matter. Including the ability to pick-up ladies of the evening. Nothing graphic, mind you, but the car does rock back and forth as your money disappears.

Loving this keyboard so far. For anyone having issues accessing the customizable key color mode, on the older models it was originally FN END, however it appears additional color schemes have been added to the more recent version. On these updated versions, the custom key color mode can be accessed with FN . When you access this mode, the tilde key will blink the color that you want to assign to a specific key. You can use FN to change the color. Once the tilde is blinking the color you want to assign, you can tap any key on the keyboard to assign the blinking color to it. You will want to set the FN key color last, since it will change itself every time you swap colors on the tilde key. Hope this helps!

I was a very happy, long-term Norton user, but I will never buy another Norton Symantec product after they coded their marketing division into their product and offshored their customer service. I have used Norton, McAffee, and Kaspersky security products Norton is the worse of the lot! I ultimately ended up going to a Kaspersky product after the Norton security suite became a massively intrusive product that seems to be more concerned with generating additional revenue for Norton Symantec as opposed to protecting your PC and seamlessly running in the background. I had Norton installed on Intel PCs and Toshiba laptops and noted performance issues, as well as, a very invasive setup routine that insisted on trying to install protection for unwanted services. I even had issues after uninstalling Norton and continued to receive unhelpful reminders" about my computers being unprotected the horror until I did a clean install of Windows 10. If you have issues with third party software I wish you good luck with Norton customer service. They hire overseas representatives that are unintelligible and are the most unhelpful people imaginable. If that is not enough reason to steer clear Norton has this sneaky automatic renewal feature if you order online, and while you can cancel it once you figure out that they charged you without authorization Norton is obviously betting that many people will not scan their charge card statements closely. And if you do not catch it, you have sixty days to call them or they will not refund your money. Remember how unhelpful their representatives are! Here is a hint for the Norton trolls the customer is always right! Focus on product function, build a wall between your marketing guys and your coders, stop trying to dog-pile the customer with intrusive notices and veiled advertisements, and be graceful with your uninstall even happy business relationships end when your customers life circumstances change.

I'll make this short. If you're an old school turn based JRPG fan who wants something with next gen graphics...this is your game! I'm 26 and I grew up playing the original Final Fantasy and Dragon Warrior games as a kid. I've stayed up to date playing virtually every RPG I could get my hands on for every console since. Lost Odyssey's story, game play, and music are all exceptional in the realm of turn-based JRPGs and it's comparable to the legendary PSX RPGs. The graphics and cutscenes are exceptional. If you have an HDTV, expect lots of eye candy and well done cutscenes...also the English voice acting is well done unlike other next gen RPGs such as Blue Dragon, Eternal Sonata, and Enchanted Arms. This game in my opinion is the best next gen RPG hands down. I'm actually sad it didn't come out to PS3 mostly because I'm console neutral I have all the new consoles and most of the old ones and I think Playstation exclusive owners are more likely to appreciate a quality JRPG than XBOX360 exclusive owners who are bigger fans of FPS games . This game deserved better in terms of sales.

I don't have the video game this keyboard is made for so I can't review that aspect. All you have to do is plug in the USB cable and windows will find the driver. If you want to program keys and create macros then you need to download the program through the logitech website. The programmable keys are along the top and sides of the keyboard. This gives you a nice choice as to where you want to place your most used keys. A special feature which is good is there is a way to turn off the function keys when game playing so you don't use them by accident. The keyboard is a little larger than a regular full desktop keyboard due to the extra programmable keys. The light for the keys can be turned on and off. There isn't any adjustment for the light intensity this is something I would like to see so if you are playing in a darkened room it is easier to see the keys. This is important when you are first using the programmable keys until you get used to where they are. The keys are slightly recessed in the center so it makes it easy and not likely for you to slip and hit another key. It is comfortable to use. Wrist guard doesn't come with it.

In days gone by, if you wanted to put some pictures and text on a piece of paper, you used Print Shop. There are lots more options these days, with better word processors and graphics programs in the bargain bin of your local software store, but you should probably still be using Print Shop. Pro Publisher does everything business stationary, greeting cards, brochures, you name it... it even includes web publishing capabilities to help novice users make their own web pages. The software is effortless to install and easy to use, and it will run on most versions of Windows without error just ignore the message about large fonts if you see it . And 200,000 images is a lot of clip art. All in all, this is a great version of Broderbund's flagship product. If you don't need the web publishing capabilities, save a few bucks and get the Deluxe version instead.

You can have the most delicious steak dinner in the world, and it means nothing if it's ruined by a bird on the rafter overhead crapping on your plate. Such is the case with these headphones. They sound great, they're comfortable, the mic is clear, good for both games and music, etc. I didn't notice the 7.1 sound too strongly, but otherwise I was extremely satisfied. Unfortunately, there are a couple of fatal design flaws. First, the the headphone jack into the phones is not detachable. Also, the connector is hard plastic not rubber, so there's no flexibility. This means the cord itself must take any force imparted between the cable and phones. Where might such force come from? That's my second design flaw. Second, the bulky USB controller is hardwired to the headphone cable only 18 or so inches from the headphones themselves. The result is that any time you pick up the headphones to put them on invariably that controller is smashing into speakers, desks, chairs, etc. This puts stress on that 18 inches of cable at both ends. The sound started getting crackly about 8 months after I got them, and know it's almost unlistenable. Unfortunately, I'm just out of warrantee, so I can't get it fixed not that I expect the issue not to reoccur . It's a shame, these are otherwise good headphones, and an excellent value now that they're down to 50.

I've been playing EA's NBA 2005 and 2006 for a few years now on my PS2. I really wanted updated rosters, and EA's NBA 2009, the last PS2 version got poor reviews, so I gave this a shot. Some of the controls and game play differs, but not significantly enough where I thought the adjustments were difficult. I have now had the game for about 15 days, and have played about 12 games. Unfortunately there are other things that I find frustrating and or disappointing. 1. Twice the game has locked-up during the first quarter. I was in the lead both times, too. NBA 2006 would occasionally lock-up while loading a game, while NBA 2005 never did. 2. Navigation of menus was extremely difficult at first. I couldn't figure out how to play the Association mode Dynasty mode in EA's NBA series, I believe . The documentation included are light at best. I ended up searching the Internet to figure it out. Now that I have it figured out, there are still a few menu design elements that could be better, such as the number of clicks required for a substitute. 3. There are a few noticeable player pronunciation issues. What gets me the most is sometimes it's correct, while others it isn't. For example, 1 3 the time we hear Nicolas Batum, and the other 2 3 we get Atom. I assume there may have been a typo, where the leading "B" was dropped atum . I noticed this on another player where part of the time we got his name and the other we got his number. NBA 2006 had 1 error I picked up where Marv Albert calls Trevor Ariza "A-riz-a" short i instead of long e, "A-reez-a" . 4. One last issue, while you will get tired of the announcers on an game, the variety of dialog and accuracy is not as good as NBA 2006. The leading scoring on the team behind at half-time could be shooting 80 - 90 , but since the team is losing he gets a bad rap. Likewise, NBA 2006 usually had a story or two for the top players on each team, while NBA 2K10 doesn't appear to have any. Bottom line, if you're looking for an updated roster on the PS2, buy it. If you're looking for an improved interface and interaction over NBA 2005 and 2006, I wouldn't waste your money -- instead, save up and get a new game console. Update March 2011 I've now completed my 2nd 29-game season and playoffs. I have moved the settings up to All-Star level for season 2 -- 1 from the highest -- and I never lost a game. I am playing the Trail Blazers and have made a few trades based on offers from other teams. I'm enjoying it enough, where I'll try the highest level for season 3 and see how it goes. I figured out some of the names pronunciation issues are based on having the players name, the players nickname, and their number. When there's no nickname, they have re-recorded the player name, and sometimes it's incorrect. The fictional players drafted have last names of existing players, so instead of always getting just a number, sometimes you will actually get their name called. While some of the season stats are better than EA, the year-to-year season tracking is non-existent. I still have the same overall impression, while fun and a nice change from EA 2005 2006, the overall game still has plenty of opportunity to improve.

I've never owned a mechanical keyboard so this is a whole new experience for me. As a professional application developer, I type all day... and night. I love it when I can control things with nothing but the keyboard it's more productive. I began to think, "Are there better keyboards out there? Ones that would make typing more enjoyable?" The construction is solid, durable, and what is expected at this price point. While typing you can definitely tell there is more room from unpressed to stricking the bottom than is found in a standard keyboard. And then their is the noise. It's much louder. Think typewriter. We also have the red backlighting. There's nothing really special there. It lights up. It's red. It has three brightness levels. This keyboard has no programmable keys. I am always pairing with other developers. There is nothing more annoying than going to someone's computer, starting to type, and realizing they changed the default shortcuts up on you. Jerk! I don't want to become dependent on macro keys. Learn the default combinations, and then stick with it. Overall, I like the durability, style, and characterstics of the keyboard. I don't regret my decision to buy it. It awaits hours of coding...

This is an update from before. I bought this not for gaming but for software programming, however I would assume that most gamers also use their keyboard for home use, or possibly even at work if they work at home like me. From a practical standpoint this keyboard is dysfunctional to me, because of the keyboard layout. I think since most gaming keyboards are also used as standard keyboards I'm knocking my rating from 4 to 3 stars, because this is applicable to all keyboards in general. The backslash keys are used often in programming, and if you work at an office, and the problem is that the keyboard layout is not standard as far as I know. There is this big Enter key where the slash key is. I type without looking except when using the symbols on the number row, or the numbers if I'm not using the ten key . That is very annoying to hit the Enter key and I have lost about an hour worth of work since I bought this. Very disappointed that they did not make it a standard layout. Other than that it's great, and I'm typing on my previous keyboard and noticing my hands are getting tired. The fonts on the number row are a bit small, so I was disappointed by that too, because that is where I want to be looking at the keyboard when I'm typing here because my keyboard is under the table in the dark. I think I'm looking for other solutions such as just putting a LED light down there.

I would rate this product 2 1 2 stars if I could I opened up a new small business and was trying to decide whether I should upgrade my Quicken 2002 deluxe to either Quicken 2003 Home and Business or the Microsoft Money equivalent. I ultimately ended up upgrading to Quicken and now think this is a mistake of epic proportion. There is good news and bad news. First the good news. The program does a lot of cool stuff that I need to do. I pay all my bills online and have never had a major problem. Occasionally, I will get the little annoyances like when all my transactions were duplicated and I had to go back and delete two years of double transaction. However, I must say that the program overall is a time saver and helps me keep track of all my expenses, both home and business. I never thought I would ever need all those little accounting details until the IRS did a complete anal exam and wanted everything in excruciating detail. Sure did come in handy. HOWEVER, for some reason, Quicken refuses to upgrade their user interface to the new millennium. There are so many features that are stuck in the 80s and 90s that it makes it more difficult to use. For example, to set preferences, you have to click the menu-preference-preference item several time to get them all. All other software brings up one preference screen with tabs in it to set all the preferences at once. And then the big beef. Here is a "suggestion" I sent today to Intuit. I will let it speak for itself. -------------------- This is not going to be a happy comment. So if you are not emotionally prepared for a very unhappy customer, please forward this message on to someone else. It won't be abusive or anything like that, but I am going to vent some of my frustrations. I just purchased Quicken 2003 for Home and Business. In general, I am pleased with the product as it should do just about everything I need it to do. BUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUT, some of the marketing that is forced in my face every time I use the program is so despicable and outright onerous that some days I just feel like throwing the whole computer out the window. Why must I put up with all the blatant advertising? No other program on the market puts users through such trials and tribulations. In fact, when I was choosing between Quicken and Money, I almost bought Money hoping that it would not subject its users to such treatment. I can't tell you how much I dislike using the program knowing that every time I look at the screen, I am going to be bombarded with advertising and marketing. I can tell you, that because of it, I will NEVER use your services. That is how much I disdain your mistreatment of customers. OK, fine. That should be enough. I think you get the idea. So my suggestion is obvious. Tone down your advertising, or at least allow the users to turn off the display of ALL advertising. For example, the little links on the bottom like "Can your business benefit from Quickbooks?" Do I feel better? Not really as I still have to endure the painful experience of these little annoyances ever time I use Quicken. I am sure I am not the only one who feels this way. PLEASE, PLEASE, PLEASE have more respect for your customers and their sanity. -------------------- Bottom line, would I buy Quicken 2003 if I had to do it all over again? Maybe, but I would investigate Microsoft Money more thoroughly first. Reading the comments about Money though, seems like there is no best solution. Good luck!

I have been a gamer since about Pac-Man, and I have seen the genre of entertainment expand. Metal Gear is one of the most interesting and provocative stories there are. It is a little bit of truth with a healthy dose of fiction. The writers for this game were wonderful, and the characters were believable. Some dialogue was a bit strange, but somehow I think that is due to translation from Japanese into American. The story makes the game. How dull would it be, and useless if you had no plot to these games? In my opinion, the best games out there are a good mix of story, dialogue, and action. Metal Gear serves this up in quantity. Also, their attempts to get the player involved as if he is Solid is wonderful. Things from Psycho Mantis's powers that he displays, to the stimulating nanomachines aka the vibrating controller makes the player feel like they are actually apart of the story. Other titles in this series accomplish this very well too, and I do not think enough games incorporate this into their games. Overall I think that this is a great game. Good action, good story, and good puzzles. One of the best treats the Playstation has to offer.

Overall, Gears of War 2 is a great buy for both hardcore gamers fans of the series and newcomers alike. If you never played the first Gears of War you'll still be able to pick this one up and quickly take part in the action. For new players, the beginning missions do offer a sort of tutorial in which you train the new rookie as a means to learn the basics for when the real fun begins. As far as the plot goes the story-line this time around is immense, filling the player in on more of what's happening between the Locust Horde and the humans. Without ruining anything there are many parts of the story that are going to really draw you into other characters' emotion. There are also other parts that are going to leave you scratching your head, not saying they're bad just more of a "Really?" kind of situation. As for the Limited Edition I'm a sucker for all the neat little things they offer in special editions and limited editions so I tend to grab those first. This limited edition includes an art book with bits and pieces of history that includes the picture you see Dom holding in the commercials of him and his girl a nice little edition that kind of makes you go "Oh cool! That's pretty awesome." Another thing it includes is a Golden Lancer for multiplayer and Horde modes. The gun itself isn't anymore powerful or anything, but does kind of set you aside in multiplayer. Not wise to be carrying around a gleaming gold gun in some of the darker maps but it's fun non the less to use. All that and a neat metal case for the game itself I think was well worth the extra 10. Gears of War 2 multiplayer is as fun as ever. Amazon here was so gracious enough to get me my game a couple days before it was slated to release thanks Amazon D yet I was still able to get on multiplayer and try it out. Included in every game doesn't have to be Limited Edition is a code to download a few maps from the first Gears of War that look better then they did in the first one and are as fun to play as they were in the first one. While joining either the COG's or Locust Horde in regular multiplayer match ups are fun as ever, I had a blast with the Horde mode. Horde mode is a new mode in which you fight wave after wave of Locust Horde to test your mettle in the heat of battle. The farthest I got was wave 25 with my cousin, but we feel we'll need a few more people if we ever plan on getting to 50. When you beat the game on any difficulty you unlock the Insane mode. A true test to the hardest of core fans of Gears of War. I remember the Insane mode was a bit easier the first time around, but I much enjoy the challenge. Word to the wise, though, you will need outside help with Insane mode. Grab another friend and see how far you can get, but this is definitely not a solo experience. Now the faults of this game, even though there are very few. First, I've noticed a few times my character getting stuck in certain spots when trying to take cover. Namely the Locust "fish" encounter that boat thing is buggy as hell . Not to turn this into a walkthrough even though I've been ranting as much no? but if and when you get up to that part watch where you take cover although you'll most likely devise the same strategy as I did I don't want to give anything away X . I also got stuck a few times playing a certain map in Horde Gridlock I believe it's called or something...one of the old maps . It's a bit frustrating when it happens but you'll quickly figure out how to "un-bug" yourself. The second thing I have a problem with are some of the achievements. I'd like to think Achievements as both hardcore gamer and casual gamer friendly when trying to get them in any game. Some are just ridiculous for the amount of time needed to spend getting them and the score you get for them. For instance there's one for killing 100,000 Horde that nets you 50 gamerscore. I played the game for about a week to two weeks between the campaign and Horde and managed to only off about 9,000 Horde. So you can only imagine how long it would take to kill 100,000 of these suckers only to get 50 gamerscore. Well that's about it. I'm sorry it was rather long. I tried to give as much info as possible without ruining anything. If you have any other questions or comments please feel free to e-mail me. Or if you picked it up and need a partner for Insane mode please e-mail me and i'll happily give you my gamertag and we can take care of that Locust Horde By the way, Coal Train is as bada as ever!

I wanted to like this game, I really did. I loved Call of Duty and played my favorite missions repeatedly. However, United Offensive just wasn't up to snuff, missing a lot of the flavor of the original game. As other reviewers have noted, single player missions are WAY too scripted - to a ridiculous extent. It's tiring, unimaginative, and not very challenging - as long as you hit the right waypoints in a mission, enemies magically appear, then stop spawning, often without reason. It lends a very artificial and mechanical feeling to the mission design, with little variation in tactical decisions even so far as to end the mission when you 'fail to follow orders' . The AI has not improved at all if anything, it's worse and your own allies constantly get in your way, blocking shots, crowding the doorways. Some new weapons are cool, like the deployable light machine guns and you can cook grenades not that the AI will ever throw them back , but these do little to compensate for the rest of the game's faults.

Out of the four adventure packs, this one is my least favorite, but it's still definitely worth playing. The Figure The actual figure that enables you to enter the level...well...I'm not entirely sure what it is. It's obviously meant to be some sort of animalistic ice carving one can make out the arms, head, and teeth with a little bridge and what looks like an igloo for a head. I think the entrace to the level in game a temple like ice structure would have been a better model. The Level Vindlevale used to be a warm, pleasant valley, until the Ice Ogres showed up. They built a giant wall to block out the sun and turn the once beautiful village into an icy wasteland. Haldor, the Northen Wizard, needs your help to reassemble The Leveler, a catapault which he plans to use to knock down the wall. The level, honestly, could be a little more challenging. It's very easy to beat, and the "boss battle" though I am glad there is one is kind of tedious. The Powerups Aside from having all the adventure packs, this is the main reason I got Empire Of Ice. The Sky Iron Shield circles around you and protects you from enemy projectiles. This one is super handy around any Cyclopses that may be throwing around axes. The Anvil Rain, which I initially wrote off I had it on Skylanders Cloud Patrol and it was totally useless is amazing in-game! A devastating rain of anvils falls from the sky, easily crushing any enemies swarming around you. I bought this loose, so I can't say much for Slam Bam. He's not a figure I'm aiming to get, as people tell me he's slow and cumbersome. You can go without this adventure pack and not be missing out too much, but it is worth it to have a complete collection.

This review is for the Soniq Boom. The selling point for me was that these headsets are USB only. Many of the higher quality headsets require both an audio and a microphone 3.5mm jack, but I don't have a microphone jack. The audio and the microphone for this headset are absolutely amazing. Once you buy it and download the drivers to your computer the drivers are also available online if you don't have a drive to install the drivers that come on a small disc , you have to spend a lot of time adjusting the settings to get it just right. The audio might be strange and your microphone audio may seem really weird and you might not be able to hear yourself at first, and the microphone mute button may not seem to work. After a couple of days, this will definitely clear up. You just have to keep adjusting the settings every time you restart your computer until it fixes itself, which only took three different uses for me. Now, my audio is clear and my microphone has no background noise whatsoever. It already has a pop filter on the inside. I can hear myself, because when you go to Playback Devices and go to the properties for the Headset, you can change how clearly you can hear your own voice, or even mute it. EDIT Broke only after a few months of limited use. Yikes....

If truth be told, being a huge fan of the original nbsp Dungeon Siege , I was more eager to play SPACE SIEGE than any other game this summer. Hyped as the next creation of Chris Taylor and Gas Powered Games both of DS fame , this was supposed to be the space age answer to the upcoming nbsp Diablo III . Well, all hell broke loose, that's for sure... The story is tissue thin but this would not have been a real problem after all, DS with its "simple peasant rising to the challenge to save the land" story was hardly...Shakespeare , if the game were to give immersion a chance. It does not. One never forgets that he is playing a video game. And the moment he might be caught in the action, the awkward controls will bring him back. I haven't seen such bad camera placement and panning ever since nbsp Neverwinter Nights 2 . Graphics is where SS shines creative and clear details everywhere. Environmental design is not after a short while the next corridor looks exactly like all the previous ones. And gives off the feeling of running in circles. The game sounds are nothing to brag about either. The explosions and weapon discharges are well done, whereas the background music is good but nowhere near DS standards I still use THAT theme as a ringtone . There is no looting fun either. Dead enemies drop generic components which can be used to buy equally generic upgrades. No sets to complete, no unique items to find, no specially crafted weapons or armor to buy. As the game progresses cybernetic augmentations get available but as the in-game NTCs keep pointing out, there are added in expense to your character's humanity. As to its length, it goes like the old Woody Allen joke on an elderly couple complaining about their rest home food "oye, it is awful!", "I know - and such small portions". Bottom line if you expect a DUNGEON SIEGE in a futuristic setting you, well, that game is not out yet. Lacking an inventory and a class system, SS is hardly a cRPG it is rather an isometric ThirdPerson shooter, similar to nbsp Shadowgrounds - Survivor nbsp or nbsp Alien Shooter Vengeance . And because these last two games do not pretend to be something they are not, in the end they are more enjoyable. If in the market for a space cRPG, try nbsp Space Hack nbsp instead. A disappointment.

I have been monitoring the development of this game since it was announced roughly three years ago. I quit playing World of Warcraft for at least couple years and had been anticipating this release with great enthusiasm. I was a DAoC player prior to WOW, so I had great faith in Mythics to deliver another great mmorpg. After the release of WAR, I played for couple weeks and got two characters to lvl 15 and lvl 17 and I had enough. I am disappointed with WAR. I played WOW for about an year and half after it was launched, and WAR only lasted me two weeks. Here is my reasons why I canceled my subscription even before my first free month 1. The graphics and environment lack personality. The graphics is not bad and not great. It is just average. I don't feel immersed in game. This is one of the major disappointments. 2. Quests in this game you can earn experience points and realm rank points. I did mostly RVR to earn both experience and realm rank points. I almost stopped doing standard quests or public quests. Standard quests are just too boring for me. Public quests were more exciting because you do it with many other players, but there weren't that many people doing public quests. I played in a high population server and had real hard time finding folks to do public quests in tier 2. So everyday I logged on to join scenarios to RVR and leveled from 1 through 17. I had just too much RVR and the game started to feel like a first person shooter game than a RPG game. This is my second major disappointment. 3. The sense of community is lacking. 99 of time I only see guild chat. There is rarely any chat in the public channel and not much chat in scenario channel. Even when a keep was under attack, no body said anything. I always called up the map and see if there was any battle going on in a keep. Even when I made jokes in public channel, no body cared. I mean seriously, this is not a single player game, say something... I was sincerely hoping that WAR would be more fun than WOW, and would bring back memories of great fun and originality of DAoC. Perhaps I set my expectation too high, and became disappointed. I just don't enjoy playing this game, and cancel the subscription.

The Good This mouse falls directly in the budget gamer pricing category, but boost some impressive features for a gaming mouse at its pricing. Programmable buttons and lighting via the provided software allow for user level customization and personalization. The software was easy to install and functional. Havit did a great job of giving introductory gamers a good experience and added some features to the mouse that those in the same pricing lack. This includes materials that prevent slipping from "sweat" and the skid pads on the bottom of the mouse to allow a more fluid movement experience. The mouse was easy to get up and running by simply plugging it in and loading the software. Without the software, the mouse uses it's default settings with the side buttons working as forward button 4 and back button 5 buttons and the scroll wheel as push down button 3. The Havit 995GT comes with a USB wireless dongle for the wireless connectivity and a USB-to-Micro USB cable for easy recharging of the mouse. The position of the charging port will allow you to both charge and use the mouse at the same time, but will require 2 USB ports if you wish to use it in that fashion. The scroll wheel and logo illuminate to show off the RBG-led color configuration. Under the scroll wheel you have the 2 DPI switch buttons that allow the user to switch the 6 DPI settings on the fly, and range from 500 DPI up to 3500 DPI 500 1000 1500 2000 2500 3500 . I found that I used the mouse at 3500 DPI to match the FPS and RTS play style that I liked. I did try the mouse on both Windows 10 and Mac OS X El Capitan in which it performed perfectly fine with the default settings. However, as the software is only for Windows, I didn't have the ability to configure the mouse while in use on the Mac OS X machine. I played a few games of Overwatch and found that it was just as responsive in movement as the Razer Deathadder 2012 model I was using in my normal play. The build quality is what is expected of a budget gaming mouse. I think, in a future model, they could improve the look and feel of the top plastics. The Bad Being a budget mouse, this has some great features. That being said, I did find a few flaws that could cause some irritations in FPS and RTS games. The lift height of the mouse is really high, and you have to lift the mouse higher then what I would expect to lose tracking of the optics. This is important with a higher DPI mouse, as it allows for a continuous movement in gameplay. I found that once I adjusted for the height that the gameplay was more fluid, but it did require some playtime for adjustment. The ability to use the mouse and charge it at the same time is a plus, but it does require the use of 2 USB port. The mouse doesn't function as a wired mouse with the charging cable plugged in, so you will have to keep the wireless receiver plugged for the mouse to function. I think if Havit had made the mouse Plug-and-Play capable, that would have been a nice addition to the features of this mouse. The Conclusion Being a budget mouse, this mouse has all a budget gamer will need and more. Even though it has some drawbacks, the mouse performed quite well. Given it's features and capabilities, it's well worth the price for those looking for budget gaming mouse. I found it was mostly comparable to my Razer Deathadder 2012 model mouse with exception to the DPI levels but I usually play my DPI at 3600, so the 100 DPI loss was not noticeable. If you're looking for a low cost but effective mid-range dpi mouse, then you may just find that the Havit 995GT Wireless Gaming mouse will fit your needs.

O.K., I got this game a few days ago thinking, "Alright, so what if it's set in WWII, again, it looks really good!" So I brought it home, all excited to play it for the first time, and I was right. It was really good. The realistic yet disturbing opening sequence was just a taste of what was to come. Imagine Saving Private Ryan turned into a video game. So I kept playing, having a great time. But there was just something missing. There were never any moments where I was like, "Oh my God, this is really awesome." like there was in Call of Duty 4. Besides the wow factor on the low side, the solo campaign was very short. I beat it just under six hours or so. Then I moved onto the multiplayer, expecting nothing less than greatness. This time, I was wrong. The multiplayer is pretty much an exact replica of the multiplayer from Call of Duty 4. Most people who read this would probably say, "Why does it matter?" Well, at first, I didn't exactly mind, but the more I played, the more I relized it just wasn't that fun. I felt like I had done it already. I just found it to be boring after playing CoD 4's multiplayer for so long. And not only does it feel like a cheap ripoff, CoD 4 did the multiplayer much better. In World at War, every time I leave a game, I get an error message saying I disconected, but I didn't. So I never see my stats after a match. Also, it's taken me nearly ten minutes to find a match sometimes, which can be extremley frustrating. And all of the perks are the perks from CoD 4 are back. Because of all these faults, I was really turned off by the multiplayer. So I went back to the solo campaign. But this time, I relized that Treyarch also copied nearly a level from CoD 4. The first time you play as the Russians, I found it to be nearly exactly like the missions from CoD 4 where you play as Captain Price. Another turn off. To me, it's basically Call of Duty 4 set in WWII with more gore and lots more curses. Though I think this is a ripoff, Treyarch did acually pulled off a decent story. It's fun, intense, and realistic. And picking Gary Oldman and Kiefer Sutherland as voices is great. Even if I think Treyarch definatly needs to work on originality, this is by far the best WWII game out there. No others can match the intensity than this one. So if your looking for a great WWII game with no originality, this is for you. If not, go with a game like Resistance 2.

We used this Antivirus for my husband's netbook. The product was easy to download, and did not really take a long time to download. The process of installing it was not the problem, however. The problem was that, once installed, it turned the computer into a total mess. With the Norton installed, my husband lost some of the programs from his computer. It deleted the software for his webcam, and several other programs. He also had to "reset" many other programs, and he had to completely redo his network and internet connection. While this product may work fine for some, it turned out to be non-fuctional for us. We had to restore the computer to the day before we put Norton on it in order to get everything back to normal. I have never been a huge fan of Norton in the past, and it looks like I will not become one anytime soon.

Things I wish I knew before buying my 3rd gen A50s. Change the mic EQ in command center to Airy for a decent mic sound streaming noise gate helps too if quiet enough Loud headset audio pops will occur when you reboot your PS4 with Dolby on. USB base station will intermittently lose power for 3-4 seconds, causing audio loss. Sometimes placing the headset into the dock to charge causes all LEDs to turn off, requiring a reset. Any WiFi 5Ghz channel on 149-164 can cause interference, including neighbors causing the issue above It's sad because these headphones have so much potential, but with a minimal manual and customer service which claims to have never heard of these issue even though the forums are full of the exact same issues . I could only recommend these to someone that has the time and effort to workaround put up with these issues. This is not a headset that will be sufficently usable straight out of the box, not for the faint hearted.

This is a very, very simple game - you match up three of a kind and try to clear the board of boxes. It's probably not the sort of game you'd play for years, but in the short term, it's hard to resist. I like the option to have ocean waves rather than music while playing. The in-game help is just right - exactly enough to tell you how to play the game without nagging. The 'relaxed' play mode is quite nice - time still counts, but you don't have to race against a clock. One feature I don't like is the hints - the game will suggest matches for you, and I'd rather figure them out for myself. No virus report from AVG here. If you're only getting the 30-minute demo as of today, the game is still free , you're doing something wrong - trust me. I've played all 100 levels and I'm halfway through the bonus 50 levels , and it's taken much more than 30 minutes to do it. It's a good thing I don't have a spouse or children to neglect, but the cats have taken to sitting in front of the computer screen.

To start off, I'll say the graphics are by far the best I've seen out of any of the Playstation 2 launch titles, and I found the controls, on the dual-shock 2 were easy to master, but there was one thing about this title that I really didn't care for, which was its multiplayer mode. So far, there haven't been any really good multiplayer games that have appeared on this system. The multiplayer mode for Armored Core 2 had to have been the worst I've seen, but Unreal Tournament wasn't much better. The amount of death match levels to choose from in 'multiplayer' mode is sad to say the least. I believe there is only seven or eight levels to choose from, and only five or six 'capture the flag' levels. One thing I did notice, which got to me even more was that all of the levels in 'one player' mode are accessible to those with an 'i-link cable', meaning I would require two to four ps2's, t.v.'s, and i-link cables to fully enjoy all the one player levels in multiplayer mode. Unless there is some sort of cheat to add more multiplayer levels, I'd say this game is a great dissapointment, because you can really only play the same levels for so long before you get sick of the sight of them. Plus, a lot of the levels to choose from are kind of boaring in appearance. Practically all of them take place indoors, and there aren't enough hazards around to keep things interesting. The pressure chamber was a nice touch, but its a shame that there weren't any living hazards as well. It does take place in space afterall. I wouldn't have minded to see monsterous renegade creatures that would attack anyone on any team, but that's getting off track. Overall, this is a good title, but don't expect a lot out of its so called 'multiplayer' mode, or you'll be dissapointed.

This really is not a photo editor, it more of an enhancer. It will make pictures look their natural best without much of a learning curve to the software. If you are a student or just dove into a new camera, and starting to lose track of your collection, it's also a great organization tool. Though it's light on features, it's great for a quick fix, and even if you screw something up, it's okay, as it keeps a copy of the original file you pulled. If you like the enhancement, and want to keep making new versions, your original will still be there. The software is quite intelligent with intuitive capability that will pull the data from your images, even older ones, and use the correct tools for that camera. The organizational aspects of this is spot on. With the information of each shot immediately available, files are much easier to sort as it begins to do that for you. I keep two external drives for my photos. One drive as the "master" source, and the other is a backup of my backup. The software works just fine with the drives. People will compare this to Lightroom and Photoshop etc., but those programs require a bit more skill and patience. And they have many more features. But for the money, it's a pretty good buy for a simple easy to use tool to clean up your collection without worries.

When Soldier of Fortune SOF first appeared, I gave it a complete miss - and it had nothing to do with BC putting a near ban on it. It seemed to me that SOF was a one trick pony Raven's GHOUL system allowed players to blow off specific limbs of the bad guys. After slaughtering wave after wave of bad guys and reaching a lame climax of the Platinum Edition of SOF, my suspicions were correct. The only thing going for SOF is its flat-out shock value, which grows tiresome and even annoyingIt's content also took on a decidedly darker tone as of September 11, 2001. The setup is that terrorists have grabbed some nuclear weapons and it's up to you, as John Mullins based on the real life soldier of fortune , to stop them. Missions take you all over the place from Tokyo to Iraq to New York's subways. It was unsettling to be marching through the New York levels mowing down bad guys and realizing that in real life worse events were unfolding. But whichever level you're currently in the over-riding goal remains the same "Neutralize" the terrorist threat. This doesn't prove to be much of a problem as the enemy AI is below the intelligence of slugs. Sure they'll pop out from around corner and fire a few shots - but these actions are scripted and not programmed into the AI. Most enemies will run at you, sometimes shooting, sometimes not. If they are shooting they won't often hit you no matter the difficulty level. If an enemy is standing still they won't aim, instead they'll spray bullets side to side making it very easy to get within about three feet before unloading your shot gun. Yes, you will get hit sometimes but there is so much extra body armor and health packs lying around that recovering is no problem. Enemies don't vary much. There are the occasional rocket launcher toting guys or tanks or helicopters but nothing to change your tactics. However, there are many points where bad guys will suddenly appear behind you to get in a few cheap shots. Which has nothing to do with the sound meter which can trigger surprise attacks if it hits its limit. They attack in numbers or with force usually just as you come around a tight corner rather than with any kind of accuracy or strategy. Level design doesn't do much to help the overall score of the game. It's linear to the extreme. You'll always know exactly where to go. Some levels require you to push buttons and turn levers - it doesn't get more complicated than that. Most levels can be done in one try. The story is clichd - direct from a bad comic book. The in-game cutscenes are pretty hokey as they rely often on shots of Mullins slowly jumping out of the way of an explosion. And even though you have a sidekick, Hawk, you never get to interact with him during missions. He goes off on his own, showing up here and there at least until he's killed . There's never the chance to coordinate attacks or at least buddy up for a while. Half-Life did it with the security guards, why can't I do it here? The graphics are all right but they are starting to show their age with the Quake 2 engine. The animation moves at a great rate on current machines and is very good. However, on the outdoor area there is an extremely annoying fog that took me back to the classic Goldeneye for the N64. Raven GHOUL engine does a good job capturing those oh so magic moments of blowing off the head of a guy from close range, or disarming a bad guy by taking off his arm with a heavy machine gun. Which is to say nothing about the kind of havoc you can create with the flamethrower. Thrown into the mix are some really good tunes that add to the tension but I could have done without the constant wailing when a bad guy gets a leg blown off. Something that I liked about SOF was the fact I couldn't pick-up every weapon I came across. Picking up the rocket launcher requires you to dump some of your weapons because it's so big. Before each mission begins you get the chance to outfit yourself with your choice of equipment and weapons. It's a bit more "realistic" that way. There's also a lack of quicksave and quickload. At the start of each section you get five saves to use at your discretion. Only once did I use more than three saves on a level. Mainly, I used saves after surprise encounters with tanks. Multiplayer is included but I found it hard to find a good multiplayer game. thanx for readin it and i hope it s a help to you, charlie x

Ultimate Shooting Collection is a collection of three shmup games by developer Milestone from 2004-2006. Milestone games are a bit of an acquired taste, putting gameplay and scoring mechanics ahead of graphical prowess. If you're looking for something to show off your Wii, this may not be the best choice. If you're a shmup fan looking for something outside the standard fare, read on. I didn't have any problems navigating the menus. I suppose if you're new to shmups and don't know the terminology it may take a bit longer to find stuff, but there aren't a lot of options so there isn't much to get lost in. Note that if you plan on playing Radirgy or Karous on a rotated screen, you'll need to enter a secret code to get them to control correctly. I have no idea why they chose to hide these. Chaos Field This game is likely the most complex of the three. There are several attack and defensive options available, enough that it may take several plays for proper use of them all to sink in. While Chaos Field may at first appear to be a boss rush game, there are a lot of subtle strategies that make the game easier to handle and scoring techniques to learn. Chaos Field gets it's name by having two "fields" of play, the normal field and the chaos field. You can switch between these at any time. The normal field has easier bullet patterns to dodge, while the chaos field is harder. But that's just scratching the surface, as they also play an important role in recharging special attacks and scoring. Generally, the normal field is used for charging up special attacks, and the chaos field is used to cash in those special attacks for killing blows and scoring huge combos. It's like an episode of Dragonball Z The normal field is all the grunting and powering up, with the chaos field being the actual fighting. Radirgy Radirgy has a cel-shaded look that takes a bit to get used to. There's a lot of text that's not really important to the plot, with cel phone messages pouring in mid-game, almost like the developers were trying to make a statement about social networking and based on the plot, perhaps they were . Playing Radirgy is all about aggression. Attacking offensively quickly recharges a bomb ABS meter, which increases your multiplier and makes you invincible for short amounts of time. The key here is to recharge the ABS before it wears off. By chaining these together, you can achieve gigantic multipliers all while completely safe from enemy fire. Once you get the rhythm down, you'll find that you're actually safer the more bullets and enemies are on screen. Karous Karous has the most unique theme of the three, featuring crows predominantly. A bit of trivia, the name Karous is a weird engrish pronunciation of how crow is spelled in Katakana. It's definitely the coolest looking game on the disc. In this game, weapons are leveled up independently from each other, by using them or collecting items. You can customize your ship this way, with better shot, sword, or shield, depending on your play style. Thus you can go balls-to-the-wall and power up the sword for lots of point-blanking, play more defensively powering up the shield, or go with a balanced approach. Also back in Karous is the recharging bomb, which has a dual purpose of absorbing bullets and leveling weapons faster. If you want to get really high weapon levels and score, learning correct bomb use is a must.

While the game is enjoyable as an adult... it has one serious problem for anyone easily offended or thinking about buying it for their kids. Microsoft who controls policy for game play will not take any action on anything said via a private message to you from any player. It may be death treaths, it may be racist hate speech, it may simply be harrassing messages of a violent sexual nature. "Nothing" will be done to players sending these messages... it is their policy that anything sent via private messages is just that... private. That being the case, I highly recommend choosing another game if you are easily offended or plan on letting your children watch and or play. The game is advertised as for ages 13 , however... due to the violent and graphic in nature messages you will recieve, the true age group is likely 21 . I enjoy playing, but have quickly filled up my allowable list of players I can "squelch" so I won't have to listen to the profanity, threats, and general harrassment.

All I can say is WOW. I am 32 years old and ever since the NES I have loved video games. In particular rpg's and strategy games. I agree with another reviewer on amazon that the recent rpg's have lost the charm and fun many had in older console generations. I bought both a Xbox360 and a Ps3 and to date, I have played more rpg's on the Xbox than the ps3. After loving the ps1 and ps2 library of solid rpg's, I have been shocked that Sony has abandoned one of its largest core gamers in this generation. I first heard about this game only recently and was happy a rpg srpg was coming to the ps3. However, there was little fanfare from Sony or the video game magazines so I wasn't expecting much. All I can simply say is that this is one of the most enjoyable games I have ever played. The story is amazing, the graphics charming, music solid, and gameplay addictive as any game in recent memory. I have owned Adobe CS6 for more than a year, and have also had LR4 installed on my computer, though haven't taken the time to really learn the features and incorporate it into my photography editing workflow. Now that I have retired and have more time on my hands to pursue my photography hobby, I want to invest in becoming much more proficient with the Adobe tools. Since Adobe will no longer be offering a non-cloud version of these essential tools, the one-year subscription to the CC Photography Plan arrived at an ideal time. I would either need to move to the cloud-based solution eventually or resolve to continue to use CS6 LR5 - these stand-alone non-cloud products will not continue to evolve, and would eventually be considered outdated. Activating the prepaid membership was simple - just go to the CC redemption web page and type in the 20-character code displayed on the card. A long list of Adobe apps appears, and you click "Install" to activate the ones covered by the subscription or "Try" to activate a limited time trial for others such as Illustrator or Dreamweaver . While Photoshop was installing, I took the time to read a brief blurb about the other available apps. I was under the misconception that the apps live in the cloud and are not installed on your computer, but that is incorrect. PS required about 1.3GB and LR about 1GB on my hard drive. The download update process required about 30 minutes. Upon installation, you are prompted to enter your credit card so that the renewal will happen automatically in one year. I decided to leave that decision for later after I've used the programs for a while. I'm sure I will receive regular prompts to supply a credit card number ! The "Learn and Support" page includes many tutorials and hints to get you started if you've never used Photoshop before. Though I've been using Photoshop Elements and Photoshop for nearly 7 years, I still feel that there is much to learn, as it is rich with features and requires a significant investment of time to become proficient. The photo editing "dabbler" will be very frustrated with Photoshop since it isn't particularly intuitive and certainly not like the Microsoft Office applications you may be comfortable with. At first glance, I didn't really see any big differences from the fairly recent CS6 version I've been using, so I googled the improvements and came up with the following list Creative Cloud Libraries Extract Assets Improved guides 3D PDF support Smart Object improvements Improved Layer Comps Blur Gallery motion effects Focus Mask Improvements to Content-Aware technology Smarter Smart Guides Desktop fonts from Adobe Typekit Font Search Expanded 3D printing capabilities Improved Windows 8.1 touch and stylus support Expanded Adobe Mercury Graphics Engine support Experimental features Intelligent upsampling, even faster Perspective Warp Adobe Generator All-new Smart Sharpen Camera Shake Reduction Adobe Camera Raw 8 and Camera Raw as a filter Extended features included Workflow time-savers It is recommended that you run the program on a computer with 8GB of RAM. Though it runs okay on my laptop with 4 GB, it is rather slow, so I will be investigating an upgrade to add RAM. Based on my experience so far, the software is feature-rich, though time-consuming and challenging to learn. However, there are many resources available on-line at the Adobe site, as well as classes and books and Youtube, to help expedite the learning curve. I'm looking forward to exploring and becoming a PS LR expert ! Though others have expressed a concern about the monthly annual subscription cost, I think it offers great value. Compare 10 month for Photoshop and LightRoom to the cost of "owning" CS6 and LR5. CS6 list price was 650 and LR5 is 110. Together they would cost you 760 to install. For the same price, you can subscribe for 76 months, and you will always have access to the latest versions of each. Adobe had been offering major upgrades to Photoshop every year or two, but you had to pay for those upgrades - usually several hundred dollars. I was concerned about being able to edit travel photos when I don't have internet access, but according to FAQs on the Adobe website, you only need to "check in" to validate the license once every 30 days. In other words, you don't have to be "in the cloud" to use Creative Cloud !

MindManager is more than just a piece of mind mapping software. As soon as you experience the ease with which you can add and manipulate words and images simply drag and drop , your actual thinking processes begin to change. You see new connections between ideas. Thoughts become tangible. Hyperlinking between maps and ideas you can create your own personal web of associations. Now, rather than making lists you can spread out your thinking on the screen and reflect on what you know and what you want to achieve. The ability of MindManager to hyperlink information comes into its own when you see the power of the export to HTML websites. You simply load up your map with your information, create links between branches you want to hyperlink and export. In no more than 30 seconds you have a fully navigable website to upload to the web or your intranet. Your branches become the web pages. The open progranmming interface is also now being exploited by third parties that add extra functions to the basic software Business Edition and Above For example at MindManuals.com we have developed a new publishing medium called a MindManual that is largely built using MindManager exported web pages.

This shampoo actually seems to be helping my incredibly dry, damaged, and brittle over-processed hair on its slow journey to recovery. Rusk Deepshine products in general have succeeded where many okay, like TONS of other brands have failed. They are gentle, helpful, have a subtle and non-annoying, beachy fragrance that fades quickly, and most importantly, they leave my hair feeling and behaving better. Here's more about that last point I have fine, but also coarse hair, super prone to frizz, but easily pushed to flat and dirty-looking by oils, leave-ins, lotions, etc. I simply can't put much of anything in my hair without it either doing nothing for the frizz, or immediately needing to be washed again or worse, and most common both . Most leave-ins make it feel, in a word, icky. Obviously, nobody leaves shampoo in their hair unless it's dry shampoo--another thing I can't use , but the shampoo is the base upon which all of your other products build. Fine, dry, tangly hair needs a shampoo that won't further damage it, or leave it feeling unclean, but also works to lay the cuticle down a bit. Conditioners help, but when you're coping with damage, you're probably going to use a conditioner with some protein, which can add to the tangly frizzy situation once the hair dries. Protein on its own makes my hair crunchy. Somehow the combination of this shampoo and either my conditioner Pureology Smooth Perfection or deep treatment RUSK Deepshine Smooth Keratin Care retains the smoothness essential to hair that like mine cannot have ANY products added again until completely dry. This regimen keeps my hair smooth and clean-feeling while drying. Once it's dry, I can add some Rusk Deepshine Lustre and a little Jele Gloss, let that set until dry, and then style. My hair feels thicker and smoother, looks shiny, and retains its clean feeling for the three days one needs to wait to shampoo again when dealing with damaged hair.

My initial review of ME3 would have probably been in the 2 star range, based entirely on the last 5 terrible minutes of the original ending to the game. I'm an ardent fan of the ME series and universe, and have read all of the books in addition to playing all of the games multiple times. So clearly I would want this review to be all love and no hate, but I can't do it. ME3 was grand throughout and drew you in emotionally. The atmosphere and things going on around you made you feel like you were literally in the midst of a terrible galactic struggle. Your choices in the prior games mattered, the relationships you developed over the course of the 3 games were fleshed out and completed. Watching EDI develop into a person was something special. The game was funny, it was sad, it made you angry. The combat was a blast and the powers finally felt like powers and were extremely useful in combat. Gun modding was back and armor and weapons choices were great, and the inventory system was far better than the 1st two games. There were amazing highs and at least one gut wrenching low in the game. The game was fantastic and drew you in emotionally... and then that abysmal ending and the last 5 minutes of the game wrecked it all. The original ending was utterly terrible, and ruled everything accomplished in all 3 games mute in an instant. I have never been so let down by an ending to anything before. I have to believe there was significant pressure by EA on Bioware to finish the game on time, and that terrible ending was a result of basically releasing the game before it was finished. Thankfully though Bioware and EA did the right thing and released the "Directors Cut" ending as a free DLC. That new ending resolved just about every complaint I had with the original ending minus minor issues I still have surrounding the "taking of the citadel" and restored my faith in Bioware and the ME franchise. It's the ending the game should have had in the first place. If it had been the ending the game came with out of the box, this and most other reviews as well, would be a 5 star review. Unfortunately the original ending was the first impression and left a bad taste in the mouth. Thank you to Bioware for coming around though and giving the game the ending it should have had, even if it came a bit late. For those that haven't tried the franchise, run, don't walk out to get all 3 games. Just be sure you install the "Directors Cut" DLC ending to ME3 before you play it so you don't have your experience wrecked by the original abysmal ending. Don't let your curiosity get the better of you to try it without it, or you'll just wish you hadn't. The Directors Cut DLC ending is a MUST!

I like the Ace Combat games, and after reading the reviews here on Amazon and on Gamefaqs I decided this was my next new Wii game. This game has some similarities to the Ace Combat series which I like, but also has it's own uniqueness which sets it apart from the Ace Combat series, and makes the game stand out on it's own as a must have game. I got some friends of mine to try it out and they were pleasently suprised how much they really liked it since they weren't really big on these types of games. So I would say this game is fun for any player whether they like these types of games or not. I would give the graphics a 4 1 2 out of 5 stars. I think the graphics are pretty awesome. When you're shooting down a plane it's pretty awesome to see pieces of airplane flying off. And sometimes you will see planes your shooting at go down in smoke which looks pretty awesome. But most of the planes you shoot up will explode into a million pieces which seems a little extravagant especially when you're just using your guns, but all the same it looks pretty awesome and leaves you very satisfied. The details of the planes look awesome too and add to the realism. The landscaping is where the graphics are kind of blah. The landscaping in the game doesn't look bad just could have used a little more detail, and more landscaping period. I think the sounds and music are deserving of 5 stars. The sounds aside from the character chatter are realistic which makes this game even better. The music is a little different from what I am used to but it sounds good and isn't hard on the ears and seems to fit perfectly with this game. The controls I would give 4 1 2 stars as well. I was quite suprised with the controls you use the remote and nunchuk in opposite hands which takes a little getting used too at first but is very easily learned, and is a fun unique way to play the game. To steer the plane you rotate the nunchuk left or right instead of using the stick which is new to me. The remote is used for throttling the speed up or down and changing targets and using the flaps, and for the special TMC move. The reason for the 4 1 2 stars for controls is because the nunchuk sometimes becomes unresponsive when you are trying to turn a different way. You can also use a gamecube controller for this game which I haven't tried yet but think it would probably eliminate this small control glitch. But like I said it's a small glitch when using the nunchuk, so it's not like it happens all the time or for long periods of time. Usually when it happens which is rarely it becomes unresponsive for about 2-3 seconds then you can usually steer the plane back to the way you want hopefully before crashing into a mountain or something. The replay value and fun factor of this game is where it shines most which easily makes this game a 5 star must have game. As for renting or buying this game I think you would be wasting money renting it since this really great game is only going for about 20 which in my opinion is a lot cheaper then what I think this game is worth.

So when it comes to mechanical keyboards, we see a lot of bloat on the market. Anything from new companies trying to make it, to existing companies trying to make it better. There are always some companies trying to just make a buck, while others strive to compete with the best. Not everything is so black and white anymore, we start to see a lot of cloned designs, re-used features, and less innovative approaches. Sometimes it's a blur of products to pick from and the best thing to take in to account before you buy is price and specifications. We all want to get what we pay for, and sometimes when we don't it leaves us a little burned. 1stPlayer has released this mechanical keyboard in three flavors, blue switches, black switches, and brown switches. The switches are 'G' switch not to be confused with Logitech's 'Romer G' switch, and little is known about them, at least by me . The diagram of the switch appears to be very similar to all cherry emulation switches, like TTC or Kaihl. The actual bump and feel --lol is different from what I am accustomed to, or enjoy. I ordered and tried the G brown switch, and to put it lightly, in comparison with my Cherry brown daily driver, there is notable differences. While it's not bad, it's not the same, nor does it feel like a brown switch... It feels more like a red to me, and is quieter still. The brown G switch fell short of what I overall expected. The keyboard features one red LED color and six modes of lighting. At this point in time I find some of the same generic modes on many keyboards across the spectrum. It's as if some of the budget companies are in a rut and using generic pre-programmed copies. While nice, I don't see general users using half of the modes as they can be distracting, or not helpful when playing or doing daily tasks. It should be noted the actual lighting and light control offer excellent levels of brightness and I was happy about this. It should also be noted that 1stPlayer had thoughtful foresight to include a cap removal key for its end users... Something I'd expect and have not seen other companies do, kudos, it's the small things that can make differences for your consumers. There is no GUI graphical user interface for going in and customizing the keyboard, no abilities to set up macros, no design feature allowing us to save anything manually to the keyboard either. I feel like this is a big miss for a gaming keyboard, branded mechanical and priced between 80-120 USD. Either the price should be lowered and the product vetted towards entry level mechanical keyboard users, or features added to make the price more adequately fit the product. The design is solid with a metal interior plate, this adds a decent weight to the keyboard and prevents sliding on solid surfaces, as well as the very nice rubber coating on the angled feet on the bottom. The feet are a nice design that have two options of elevation... I thought this was smart, a good design implication I was very happy with. I'd also like to see a USB pass through added to this board, and I believe this would further increase its value. If you are someone who has used a keyboard with a pass through then you understand its value and likely agree with me. All said and done, the keyboard is nice, but leaves some things to be desired. Very fun game. First, like my experience with the other launch games, you have to play it for a few minutes before you really see the next gen graphic difference. This game does look amazing. Single player is great, although over half way through it kind of loses a little steam. The online MP is a lot of fun, depending on the game modes you play. While its no BF4, but some of the things I think it does do better. You have access to all the rifles right from the beginning, and complete challenges to get scopes, attachments and other things. The 3 classes available each have special abilities to use in MP which are all great, and you can level those abilities up as well. Maps, while not as large as BF4, are a lot of fun and sometimes very intense. The game has worked very well right out of the package with no real server issues that I have run into. EDIT After having this game for a few months now, I am somewhat disappointed with it. Visuals are great, when the frame rate doesn't take a dive online. Server issues were horrific at a few points, and GG was pretty slow to respond to the game breaking issues the first time over a month before they even acknowledged it the first time, and the lack of content so far is pretty sad. It just got two new maps for free which is nice, but after putting the game down for a few weeks, I jumped on to try the new maps out and only played each one once and haven't touched the game since. I have paid for the season pass, and since no content has come out for it, and other games are coming out, I probably won't go back to playing it.

I don't know of any other popular home finance packages, so I am stuck with Quicken. Every time I am forced to upgrade, I hope that the new version will be better. It is always slightly worse. For example, in the previous version, to reconcile a checking or credit card account, you simply clicked the 'Reconcile' button above the register. In the 2013 version, this functionality is hidden under a tiny, unnamed menu with a gear icon, in the corner of the window. By the way, the font size setting for the register as a whole does not work to make the text smaller. I also hoped that Quicken would be more intelligent about how it handled company-match inputs to 401Ks, HSAs, etc. It is no better. If your pay amount varies from check to check, you must manually update the company match figure. Since all ? companies structure their contribution as "X match, up to Y of your pay," and have done so for many years, Intuit should incorporate this as an automatic calculation. Quicken's habit of inserting little ads for add-on products like their affiliate credit cards is also very unacceptable for boxed-type software.

I have to admit that I was completely suprised at how much I enjoy my Nintendo DS. The advances Nintendo has made over its "GameBoy Advanced" are amazing. In fact, just the "mini" games in Super Mario 64 game has more entertainment value than almost any Gameboy Advanced Game. Another thing that is incredible is the size of the game... it is the size of a standard flash memory card, which makes Nintendo DS games about 75 smaller than those for GB Adv. Here are a run down of a few of my points. - The touch screen incorporates very well into the games. In fact, you start to wonder,"What did I do before a touch screen?" - The power of the DS is very similar to the Nintendo 64 - and in my opinion- you really don't need much more. Have games really improved all that much since the 64? The graphics may be a little better with the bigger machines... but game play has stayed about the same. - The ability to link wirelessly may be a double edged sword. While it will get rid of those burdensome wires and allow for text picture chatting it may eventually get the DS banned on airplanes. The Feds are not big fans of having anything that transmits a signal on an airplane... so stay tuned.... because this may become a MAJOR drawback to the DS. - The text and picture chatting... I guess this may be cool if you are a teenager, but I doubt if I will ever use it. As a whole - I would completely recommend the DS for video games players of all ages! I believe that Nintendo has done a wonderful job in creating a small but powerful video game device!

The Wasteland is huge so you probably won't visit every area on the map on your first play-through. There's a great variety in the skills you can give your character you can have a "soldier" character who excels in bringing down foes with every gun in the game or a "ninja" character who excels in stealth, melee and unarmed combat. The Fallout 3 web site has some "recipes" for character builds that's good to read before building a character. In Fallout 3, your character can "hack" computer terminals by selecting words from a memory dump on the computer terminal. Normally you can make up to 4 attempts. What I did not know is that you can make THREE attempts then press the Red button on the terminal to cancel that "session". This way you can access the terminal again. You can keep doing this as long as you never go beyond the maximum 4 attempts. You will WANT to be able to unlock every terminal because it may result in deactivating some turrets or activating a Protectron guard that will help you with enemies. Also, as you explore buildings and underground terrain, you will encounter many locked doors that will require a high "lockpick" skill to open them. You will want to keep track of these. These don't always contain good loot but sometimes they do e.g., the power fist . If your "lockpick" skill is high enough e.g., 80 or more and you run out of bobby pins, you can simply the force the lock and usually it will open. If you like gratuitous blood and gore, there's a "perk" that your character can earn to significantly increase the amount of blood and gore that is displayed when your character kills an enemy. I found that to be extremely fun initially but after doing it a dozen times, it kind of felt too over the top. Your enemy literally explodes into many small bits of flesh. Sometimes, the bits are too small to find so it takes a while to find one to loot your enemy. The quests in Fallout 3 contain one or more Optional objectives. These optional objectives are more difficult but are definitely worth attaining. For example, Moira Brown's quests don't look very interesting at first but for example one of the quests allows your character to gain a useful ability that will enhance survival. The difficulty of the enemies you encounter in the Wasteland increases as your character levels up. I don't recommend that you level up too quickly. Try to explore the Wasteland while you are still at a low level because at higher levels, the really big enemies start appearing and they will make exploring outdoors dangerous. Eventually, your character will have a home. I bought the "Vault" house theme and the jukebox for around 1500 or 2000 caps but sadly they were NOT worth the money. Save the money for guns and ammo e.g., Fat Man and Mini Nukes or stimpaks. Save all the Nuka Cola bottles, Nuka Cola Quantum bottles, Holo-tags and Pre-war books you find. Resist the urge to drink or sell them. Try not to press the "E" key all the time because if an item is in the crosshairs and the item's label is red, your character will instantly grab the item and this will anger any NPCs next to your character. Moira Brown's shop is littered with items. I made the mistake of hitting "E" and my character grabbed something off the counter. Afterwards, Moira's guard started shooting at my character. It's kind of ridiculous but I guess there's no way for the game to distinguish between casually picking something up to look at it and stealing something. For me, the biggest pluses of this game are the appearance of the outdoor environments, NPCs and buildings. Everything looks beat up and run down and it all fits with the game's setting. The music from your Pip Boy is ironic but really catchy. It's great when the song "Butcher" plays while you're mowing down super mutants with a minigun. The irradiated monsters and NPCs in the game are truly grotesque and many of them just lunge at you. I did not became comfortable seeing a centaur even after seeing so many of them. The creature design lends a lot to the feeling of danger when your character is out in the Wasteland. These elements all effectively build up the feeling of immersion in the in-game world. This game is a 5 out of 5.
